---
title: "Atmospheric measurements reveal underestimated HFC emissions from the UK"
date: 2025-03-12
author:
  - name: "Jane Smith"
    affiliation: "National Physical Laboratory"
  - name: "Robert Jones"
    affiliation: "University of Bristol"
  - name: "Sarah Williams"
    affiliation: "Met Office"
categories:
  - HFCs
  - atmospheric inversion
  - UK emissions
  - inventory verification
image: figures/hfc-trend-thumbnail.png
description: >
  New atmospheric measurements and inversion modelling show that UK emissions of
  hydrofluorocarbons are significantly higher than reported in national inventories,
  with implications for climate targets and international reporting.
---

::: {.pdf-download-bar}
**Download this page as a PDF policy brief:**
[Download PDF](uk-hfc-emissions.pdf){.btn .btn-sm .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.highlight-meta}
**Authors:** Jane Smith (NPL) · Robert Jones (University of Bristol) · Sarah Williams (Met Office) \
**Published:** 12 March 2025 \
**Related publication:** Smith et al. (2025), *Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics* — [doi:10.XXXX/XXXXX](#)
:::

## Background

Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) are a family of synthetic greenhouse gases used widely
in refrigeration, air conditioning, and heat pumps. Although they do not deplete
the ozone layer, many HFCs have global warming potentials hundreds to thousands of
times greater than carbon dioxide over a 100-year timescale. Their regulation is
governed by the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol, which requires countries
to progressively reduce HFC production and consumption.

Countries report HFC emissions annually to the United Nations Framework Convention
on Climate Change (UNFCCC) using a bottom-up approach: they multiply activity data
(amounts of gas sold, equipment in use) by emission factors derived from laboratory
experiments or engineering assumptions. The accuracy of these estimates is difficult
to verify independently.

## What we did

Using continuous atmospheric measurements from three UK monitoring stations — Mace Head
(Ireland), Tacolneston (Norfolk), and Bilsdale (Yorkshire) — combined with backward
atmospheric transport simulations from the Met Office NAME model, we estimated UK HFC
emissions using an inverse modelling framework for the period 2015–2024.

Our method works by detecting the enhanced concentrations of HFCs in air masses
that have passed over the UK before arriving at our measurement sites. By combining
these observations with knowledge of how the atmosphere transports and dilutes trace
gases, we can infer the strength of UK emissions.

![Atmospheric HFC concentrations measured at Tacolneston, 2015–2024. Elevated episodes correspond to air arriving from the UK. Trend line shows the long-term baseline.](figures/hfc-timeseries.png){fig-alt="Time series of HFC-134a concentrations at Tacolneston showing episodic enhancements and a rising baseline."}

## Key findings

Our analysis shows that:

1. **UK HFC emissions are 30–50% higher than reported** in the national inventory
   for the majority of years in our study period, with the discrepancy growing over
   time.

2. **HFC-134a and HFC-125** account for the largest portion of the gap, suggesting
   under-reporting in the refrigeration and mobile air-conditioning sectors.

3. **Emissions are not declining as fast as inventories suggest.** While the
   official inventory shows a 15% reduction since 2018, our atmospheric estimates
   indicate a reduction of only 4–7% over the same period.

![Comparison of GEMMA atmospheric inversion estimates (blue) with UK UNFCCC inventory submissions (orange), 2015–2024. Shaded bands show 95% uncertainty ranges.](figures/hfc-comparison.png){fig-alt="Bar chart comparing atmospheric inversion estimates with inventory submissions. Inversion estimates are consistently higher."}

::: {.callout-important}
## Key figure
Our central estimate is that UK HFC emissions in 2024 were **8.5 Mt CO₂-equivalent**
(95% range: 7.1–10.2 Mt CO₂e), compared with the reported inventory figure of
**5.9 Mt CO₂e**. This difference is equivalent to the annual emissions of
approximately 600,000 UK cars.
:::

## Why the gap exists

Several factors may explain the discrepancy between atmospheric and inventory estimates:

- **Fugitive emissions from ageing equipment** may be poorly captured by activity-based
  methods, which tend to assume average leak rates.
- **Illegal imports** of refrigerants, documented by enforcement agencies, may not
  appear in sales statistics.
- **Methodology updates** in inventory compilation can sometimes mask real trends.

We emphasise that the gap does not necessarily imply deliberate under-reporting.
Bottom-up inventory methods have known limitations, and our results are consistent
with discrepancies found for other countries using similar atmospheric approaches.

## Policy implications

These findings have several implications for UK climate policy and international
reporting:

- Independent, measurement-based verification of greenhouse gas inventories is
  feasible and provides important quality assurance.
- Strengthening enforcement of HFC import regulations could both reduce emissions
  and improve inventory accuracy.
- The UK may wish to review its activity data and emission factors for the
  refrigeration sector in consultation with industry.
- Investment in long-term atmospheric monitoring networks provides high-value
  information for inventory verification at relatively low cost.

## Methods and uncertainties

Full methodological details are given in Smith et al. (2025). Key points:

- Measurements are made using gas chromatography with mass-spectrometric detection,
  calibrated against AGAGE (Advanced Global Atmospheric Gases Experiment) standards.
- Atmospheric transport is simulated using the Met Office NAME Lagrangian dispersion
  model driven by operational meteorological analyses.
- Emission estimates are produced using a hierarchical Bayesian inversion framework.
- Uncertainties (shown as shaded bands in figures) account for measurement
  uncertainty, transport model uncertainty, and prior uncertainty in spatial
  emission patterns.

## Further reading

- Smith J et al. (2025). Atmospheric-based estimates of UK HFC emissions, 2015–2024.
  *Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics*. [doi:10.XXXX/XXXXX](#)
- Simmonds PG et al. (2020). Atmospheric observations of HFCs. *Nature*.
- BEIS (2024). UK greenhouse gas inventory 1990–2023. [link](#)
